questions.question: Explain the if-else statement in Java with a suitable example. Also explain the syntax of nested if-else.
questions.hint: Remember to mention that only one block executes based on the first true condition
keyPoints: if statement checks a boolean condition
if condition is true, execute block inside braces
else block executes when condition is false
else-if ladder for multiple conditions
Nested if-else allows checking conditions within other conditions
Example showing grade calculation based on marks

questions.question: Explain the for loop in Java with its different forms. Also explain the enhanced for loop (for-each).
questions.hint: Enhanced for loop cannot modify array elements directly
keyPoints: for loop has three parts: initialization, condition, iteration
Syntax: for(initialization; condition; iteration)
Loop executes as long as condition is true
Enhanced for loop iterates over arrays and collections
Syntax: for(Type var : array/collection)
Example showing sum of array elements

questions.question: Explain while and do-while loops in Java. Differentiate between them with examples.
questions.hint: Remember the semicolon after do-while condition
keyPoints: while loop checks condition before executing body
do-while executes body at least once before checking condition
while: condition checked first, may not execute body
do-while: body executes at least once guaranteed
Syntax differences between while and do-while
Use case: menu-driven programs prefer do-while

questions.question: Explain the switch statement in Java. Discuss its syntax, rules, and the use of break statement.
questions.hint: Java 14+ supports switch expressions with yield keyword
keyPoints: switch selects one of many code blocks based on value
Expression must be byte, short, int, char, String, or enum
case labels must be constants or literals
break terminates the switch block
Without break, execution continues to next case (fall-through)
default case executes when no match found
Switch is more efficient than if-else-if for multiple choices

questions.question: Explain break and continue statements in Java with examples. How are they used in loops?
questions.hint: break exits the loop completely, continue only skips one iteration
keyPoints: break terminates the loop or switch immediately
When break executes, control exits the innermost loop/switch
continue skips current iteration and proceeds to next
continue transfers control to loop's update expression
Labeled break/continue used with nested loops
Example showing even number printing using continue

questions.question: Explain how control statements (if-else, for, while) are used in JSP using scriptlets. Provide a complete example demonstrating conditional rendering and loop iteration in a JSP page.
questions.hint: Use request.getParameter() to get parameters and response.setContentType() for proper rendering
keyPoints: JSP scriptlets use <% %> syntax to embed Java code
Control statements work identically to Java within scriptlets
if-else used for conditional HTML generation
for/while loops used to iterate over collections or generate repeated HTML
Example should show: checking parameter value and displaying appropriate message
Example should show: iterating over array/list and displaying items in HTML table
Mention that scriptlets mix business logic with presentation (not recommended)

questions.question: Compare the implementation of control statements in traditional JSP scriptlets versus JSTL tags. Explain why JSTL is preferred in modern JSP development.
questions.hint: JSTL requires taglib directive: <%@ taglib prefix='c' uri='http://java.sun.com/jsp/jstl/core' %>
keyPoints: Scriptlets use <% if(condition) { %> syntax, JSTL uses <c:if test='${condition}'>
Scriptlets require Java knowledge, JSTL uses tag-based syntax
JSTL separates business logic from presentation clearly
JSTL is easier to read for web designers unfamiliar with Java
Scriptlets make debugging difficult, JSTL provides better error messages
JSTL supports EL (Expression Language) for accessing data
JSTL provides: <c:if>, <c:choose>, <c:forEach>, <c:forTokens>
Modern best practice: avoid scriptlets, use JSTL or JSP tags

questions.question: Explain the JSTL core tags for control flow: <c:if>, <c:choose>, <c:forEach>. Provide syntax and examples for each.
questions.hint: Remember to include JSTL core taglib directive in JSP
keyPoints: <c:if>: conditional execution with test attribute
<c:if> syntax: <c:if test='${condition}'> body </c:if>
<c:choose>: multi-conditional branching like switch
<c:when> and <c:otherwise> used within <c:choose>
<c:forEach>: iteration over collections, arrays, ranges
<c:forEach> syntax with var, items, begin, end, step attributes
<c:forTokens>: tokenize strings using delimiter
Example showing each tag in practical scenarios

questions.question: Design a JSP page that reads a parameter 'action' from the request and uses control statements to: (a) Display 'Welcome' for action='login', (b) Display 'Goodbye' for action='logout', (c) Display 'Invalid' for any other value. Use both scriptlet approach and JSTL approach.
questions.hint: Use ${param.action} in EL to access request parameters
keyPoints: Use request.getParameter('action') to get parameter value
Scriptlet approach: if-else with String comparison using equals()
Scriptlet: <% String action = request.getParameter('action'); if('login'.equals(action)){ %>
JSTL approach: <c:choose> with <c:when test="${param.action == 'login'}">
Default case handled in else or <c:otherwise>
Show complete code for both approaches
Mention that null check may be needed for missing parameter

questions.question: Write a Java program using control statements to calculate the sum of first 20 natural numbers using a for loop. Trace the execution and show the final output.
questions.hint: Formula for sum of first n natural numbers: n(n+1)/2
keyPoints: Initialize sum = 0 before loop
for loop: for(int i = 1; i <= 20; i++)
Each iteration: sum = sum + i
Iteration 1: sum = 0 + 1 = 1
Iteration 2: sum = 1 + 2 = 3
Continue pattern: sum follows n(n+1)/2 formula
Final output: sum = 210
Show complete Java code with System.out.println

questions.question:
Write a program using nested for loops to generate the following pattern:
*
**
***
****
*****

Trace the execution for first 3 iterations showing which loop controls rows and which controls columns.
questions.hint: Use System.out.print() for stars and System.out.println() for newlines
keyPoints: Outer loop controls rows (i from 1 to 5)
Inner loop controls columns (j from 1 to i)
Print '*' without newline inside inner loop
Print newline after inner loop completes
Row 1: i=1, inner loop runs j=1, prints '*'
Row 2: i=2, inner loop runs j=1,2, prints '**'
Row 3: i=3, inner loop runs j=1,2,3, prints '***'
Complete pattern matches the triangle

questions.question: Explain the try-catch-finally control structure in Java. How does it differ from try-with-resources? Provide syntax.
questions.hint: At least one catch or finally block must follow try
keyPoints: try block contains code that may throw exception
catch block handles specific exception types
Multiple catch blocks possible (most specific to general)
finally block always executes regardless of exception
finally used for cleanup: closing files, connections
try-with-resources: AutoCloseable resources closed automatically
Syntax: try(resource) { } catches and handles exceptions

questions.question: What is the difference between = and == operators in Java? Explain with examples. Also explain the importance of proper comparison in control statements.
questions.hint: In if(a = b), a gets value of b, and that value is evaluated as boolean
keyPoints: = is assignment operator, == is comparison operator
Assignment returns the assigned value
Comparison returns boolean (true/false)
Common mistake: using = instead of == in conditions
For objects, == compares references, equals() compares content
String comparison should use .equals() method
Control statements require boolean expressions

questions.question: Consider a servlet that processes login requests. Write Java code using control statements to validate: username is not empty, password is at least 6 characters, and display appropriate error messages. Use if-else-if ladder.
questions.hint: Use request.setAttribute() to pass error messages to JSP
keyPoints: Get parameters: String user = request.getParameter('username')
Get parameters: String pwd = request.getParameter('password')
Check if username is null or empty using equals() or isEmpty()
Check password length using .length() method
Use if-else-if for priority validation
Set request attributes for error messages
Forward to JSP using RequestDispatcher
Code should handle null safely using null checks

questions.question: Explain how control flow works in a servlet's service() method. How does the servlet container control the lifecycle? Discuss doGet(), doPost() and how conditional processing is handled.
questions.hint: Override doGet/doPost, not service() unless handling all methods similarly
keyPoints: Servlet container calls service() for each request
service() determines HTTP method and calls appropriate doXxx()
doGet() handles GET requests, doPost() handles POST requests
Control flow: service() -> doGet()/doPost() -> business logic
Servlet lifecycle: init() -> service() repeatedly -> destroy()
Multiple threads can execute service() simultaneously
Control statements in doGet/doPost process request parameters
Response generation uses PrintWriter or ServletOutputStream
